Isa.41.10: The triple imperative — fear thou not... be not dismayed... I will help thee — anchors Israel's confidence not in their circumstances but in their covenant God. The Hebrew tsedeq ('righteousness') here speaks of God's faithful, saving justice; His right hand of righteousness upholds because He must keep His own promises. Believers cling to this verse for comfort, but its first force is theological: God's people are kept because God is who He says He is.
Rom.8.29: Foreknew (proegnō) here is not bare prescience but covenantal love set on a person before time — the same usage as Amos 3:2. Predestination is not arbitrary but Christological: God's eternal goal is conforming a chosen people to the image of His Son. The order of the golden chain (vv. 29–30) is unbroken; what God begins, He finishes.
Isa.44.3: The promise of pouring water on the thirsty is paired with pouring God's Spirit on Israel's offspring — physical refreshment as a picture of spiritual regeneration. Joel 2:28 develops this; Pentecost (Acts 2) inaugurates it. The agent of revival is always the sovereign God Himself.
Rom.12.2: Be not conformed (syschēmatizesthe, present passive) — stop letting the world press you into its mold. Be transformed (metamorphousthe) — undergo continual inward change by renewing your mind through the Word. The result is the believer's discernment of God's will, which is good, well-pleasing, and perfect.
John.3.16: Jesus' words (still red-letter, despite the chapter break) state the gospel in compressed form: God's love is the source, the giving of His unique Son is the price, faith is the instrument, and everlasting life is the gift. 'World' (kosmos) here speaks of humanity in its fallenness — not every individual indiscriminately saved, but no ethnic boundary on the offer. The verse declares both the universal scope of the gospel and the particular condition: whoever believes.
Phil.4.13: Often weaponized as a self-help slogan, this verse means the opposite. Paul has just said he has learned contentment in plenty and in want (vv. 11–12); 'all things' refers to all those circumstances. Christ strengthens His servants to be content in His providential placement, not to achieve any ambition they choose.
Jer.29.11: God speaks this to exiled Israel headed into seventy years of Babylon — the 'plans for welfare' come after a generation of judgment. American Christians often rip this verse from context as a personal life-promise; in fact it is a covenantal pledge to a chastened nation. The principle holds: God's plans for His people serve their ultimate good even through painful means.
Prov.3.5: Trust (batach) means to lean the full weight of one's life upon the LORD as a refuge. The contrast — lean not unto thine own understanding — is not anti-intellectual but anti-autonomous: human reason apart from divine revelation is unreliable. Wisdom begins where self-confidence ends.
Matt.6.33: Jesus contrasts Gentile anxiety over food and clothing (vv. 31–32) with kingdom-priority living. Seek (zēteō) is present imperative — keep on seeking — and 'first' is qualitative, not merely chronological. When God's reign and righteousness become the controlling pursuit, the Father takes responsibility for everything else.
Rom.8.28: All things — including suffering, persecution, death (vv. 35–39) — work together by God's sovereign agency. The promise is restricted to those who love God, defined immediately as 'the called according to His purpose' — i.e., the elect. The 'good' is not earthly comfort but conformity to Christ (v. 29).
Phil.4.6: Be careful for nothing — the imperative is absolute, every anxiety. The remedy is fourfold: prayer (general God-ward speech), supplication (specific requests), thanksgiving (the posture), and making known one's requests. The result (v. 7) is the supernatural peace that guards heart and mind in Christ.
John.14.6: Three articles in the Greek — the way, the truth, the life — lock the claim down: Jesus is not a way but the way; not religion but the only Way. The exclusivity is offensive in pluralist cultures; it is also the gospel. No man cometh to the Father — the bridge from sinner to God is one Person, His Son.
2Cor.5.17: In Christ — the most defining phrase in Pauline theology, denoting union with Christ. New creation (kainē ktisis) is not improvement but inauguration of the eschatological age in the believer (Isa 65:17, Rev 21). Conversion is not turning over a new leaf; it is being remade.
Prov.4.23: The heart in Hebrew thought is the seat of will, intellect, and affection — not merely emotion. 'Above all keeping' (mishmar) — every other watchpost matters less. From the heart flow the issues of life, so its corruption pollutes everything downstream.
Gen.1.1: The Bible's foundational sentence asserts a personal God, an absolute beginning, and ex nihilo creation. The Hebrew bara' (created) takes God as its only subject — only God creates. Reject this verse and every doctrine downstream collapses; embrace it and every other doctrine has a footing.
2Cor.6.16: Paul presses the unequally-yoked principle (v. 14) to its temple climax. Believers corporately are the temple (naos) of the living God — His indwelling presence is not in stones but in His blood-bought people. The covenant promise of Lev 26 and Ezek 37 is inaugurated in the church.
Josh.23.10: Joshua's farewell speech reminds Israel that the LORD their God fights for them — one of you can chase a thousand because the battle has never been theirs. The reciprocal obligation (v. 11) is to love the LORD; covenant power flows along covenant fidelity.
Isa.40.31: They that wait (qavah) — those who actively trust in God's covenantal action. The renewal pictured is not human stamina but divine substitution of strength. Mounting up like eagles, running without weariness, walking without fainting describe the lifelong rhythm of faith.
John.1.1: Three statements: the Word's eternity (in the beginning was), distinction from the Father (with God), and full deity (was God). Greek word order — theos en ho logos — places theos forward emphatically, ruling out 'a god.' The Logos is co-eternal, co-essential, and personally distinct.
Rom.8.31: What then shall we say to these things? — to the entire chain of vv. 28–30. If God is for us — and He is, demonstrably, by the cross — no opposing force has decisive weight. The Christian's security is not in himself but in the One who is for him.
Luke.11.9: The three present imperatives — keep on asking, keep on seeking, keep on knocking — emphasize persistence in prayer. Verses 11–13 ground the promise in the Father's character: how much more shall your heavenly Father give the Holy Spirit. The greatest gift is the Giver Himself.
2Cor.12.9: My grace is sufficient — present tense, ongoing reality — answers Paul's three-fold prayer for the thorn's removal. God's strength (dynamis) is perfected (teleitai, brought to its goal) in human weakness (astheneia). Hence Paul's paradoxical glorying: when I am weak, then am I strong.
Matt.7.7: In context the asking is for daily bread (6:11) and forgiveness (6:12), framed by the Father's character (vv. 9–11). Prayer is not a bargaining mechanism but a child's confidence in his Father. The promise is unconditional: every one that asketh receiveth.
1Sam.15.22: Samuel's rebuke of Saul: ritual obedience without heart obedience is rejected. To obey is better than sacrifice — God values the inward submission of the will over the outward performance of religion. This is the prophetic critique that runs from here through Hosea 6:6 to Matthew 23.
Isa.43.25: I, even I, am he — the divine I AM repeats Himself for emphasis. He blots out (machah) transgressions for His own sake — God's covenant fidelity, not Israel's worthiness, is the engine of forgiveness. The grounds of pardon are always grace.
Isa.61.1: Jesus reads this passage in the synagogue at Nazareth (Luke 4:18–21) and announces its fulfillment in Himself. The anointing of the Spirit equips the Servant for proclaiming gospel-good-news (basar) to the afflicted, healing the brokenhearted, and announcing the favorable year of the LORD.
Heb.8.10: The author cites Jeremiah 31:33: I will put my laws into their mind and write them in their hearts. The new covenant differs from the old not in standard but in interior agency — God Himself effects the obedience. This is regeneration in covenantal terms.
Heb.13.6: The author quotes Psalm 118:6 to encourage persecuted believers. The LORD is my helper (boēthos) — the answer to the fear of man is the fact of God's covenantal presence. What can man do unto me — practically, much; ultimately, nothing that touches the soul.
Gal.6.9: Be not weary (mē enkakōmen) — do not lose heart in well-doing. The harvest principle is invariable: in due season we shall reap. The spiritual law of sowing and reaping (vv. 7–8) governs the Christian life as surely as it governs agriculture.
1Cor.16.13: Four staccato imperatives: watch ye, stand fast in the faith, quit you like men, be strong. Paul's military cadence calls the Corinthians to vigilance, doctrinal stability, courage, and strength — every virtue grounded in v. 14: let all your things be done with charity.
Matt.24.35: Heaven and earth shall pass away — including the present cosmic order at the consummation (2 Pet 3:10–13). My words shall not pass away — Jesus' authority outlasts creation itself. This is the strongest possible claim to verbal inspiration of His own teaching.
Gal.5.22: Fruit (singular) of the Spirit, not fruits — one ninefold cluster grows from one source. These are not natural temperament traits but supernatural produce of the Spirit's indwelling. The list contrasts the works of the flesh (vv. 19–21) item-by-item.
Ps.23.1: David's confession opens a thousand sermons. The LORD (Yahweh) is my shepherd (ro'i) — covenant name plus pastoral metaphor. The corollary is immediate: I shall not want — every legitimate need is met by the One who shepherds.
1Cor.15.58: The therefore (hōste) anchors the whole resurrection chapter. Because Christ is risen and we shall be raised, our labor in the Lord is not in vain — every faithful service has eschatological weight. Stand steadfast, unmoveable, and abound; this is the only sane response to the resurrection.
Jer.33.3: Call (qara') intensively — God invites the prophet to summon Him. Great and mighty things which thou knowest not (betsuroth, 'inaccessible things') — God reveals what man cannot discover. The promise is for the prophet under siege but applies to every believer in prayer.
Phil.1.6: He who began (enarxamenos) — God Himself initiated the work. Will perfect (epitelesei) — He guarantees its completion at the day of Jesus Christ. Sanctification is monergistic in its source even though it engages our cooperation; what God starts, He finishes.
Eph.2.10: We are His workmanship (poiēma — His artistry); created (ktisthentes — divine, not natural birth); in Christ Jesus (the sphere); unto good works (the goal); which God before ordained (predestined the very works). Salvation is by grace through faith (v. 8) for a life of pre-prepared good works.
Ps.1.1: The Psalter opens with a beatitude. Three progressive verbs — walked, stood, sat — trace the descent into wickedness; the blessed man refuses each step. The contrast between the two ways (the law-rooted tree of v. 3 vs. the wind-blown chaff of v. 4) frames the whole Psalter.
1Pet.2.9: Peter applies four titles given to Israel (Exod 19:5–6, Isa 43:20–21) directly to the church: chosen race, royal priesthood, holy nation, peculiar people. The result clause: that ye should show forth the praises (aretas, virtues) of Him who called you out of darkness. The church is missional by ontology.
Eph.6.10: Be strong (endynamousthe, present passive) in the Lord — the strength is given, not produced. The Christian's warfare is not against flesh and blood (v. 12); the resources must be from beyond flesh and blood. The armor of God (vv. 13–17) follows.
1Chr.28.9: David's charge to Solomon: know God, serve Him with a perfect (shalem, whole) heart, and a willing mind. The LORD searcheth all hearts — divine omniscience is moral, not merely cognitive. Forsake Him, He will cast off forever — the warning is real even within covenant assurance.
Josh.1.9: The fourth divine charge to be strong and of good courage. Be not afraid, neither be thou dismayed — twin commands grounded in the LORD thy God is with thee whithersoever thou goest. The leader's courage flows from God's presence, not his own resources.
2Tim.1.7: God hath not given (edōken) us the spirit of fear (deilias, cowardice) — paralyzed timidity is not the Spirit's gift. Rather, three: power (dynamis), love (agapē), and a sound mind (sōphronismos, disciplined self-control). The triad equips a young pastor for hostile times.
Isa.45.17: Israel shall be saved with an everlasting salvation — the doctrine of perseverance grounded in God's covenant. World without end — the salvation has no expiration. Ye shall not be ashamed nor confounded — the eschatological vindication of God's people.
John.15.7: If ye abide in me — present subjunctive, ongoing union — and my words abide in you. The condition is mutual indwelling: the believer in Christ, Christ's words in the believer. Then the prayer-life takes on a new accuracy: ye shall ask what ye will, and it shall be done.
1Pet.5.7: Casting (epirhipsantes, aorist participle of decisive action) — once and for all hand over your anxieties. He careth for you — the present indicative grounds the imperative. Care is shed because the Father cares.
Isa.55.6: Seek (darash) the LORD while He may be found — the day of grace has limits. Call (qara') upon Him while He is near. The Hebrew imperatives are urgent because the season of accessibility is finite.
Ps.27.1: Three covenantal claims: the LORD is my light, my salvation, the strength of my life. Each excludes a corresponding fear. David grounds his fearlessness not in his stoutness but in the LORD's identity.
Eph.3.20: Now unto Him who is able (dynamenō) — present participle, ongoing capacity — to do exceeding abundantly above (hyper ekperissou) all that we ask or think. Compound after compound piles up Greek superlatives. The measure is not our request but His indwelling power.
Isa.59.21: This my covenant — Yahweh's own promise of permanent prophetic continuity. My Spirit and my words — the means of divine self-disclosure — are guaranteed to remain on God's covenant people forever. The verse undergirds the doctrine of canon and the perpetuity of the Word.
Jer.30.18: I will bring again the captivity — God promises restoration to the exiles. The city shall be builded upon her own heap (tel) — Jerusalem rebuilt on its own ruins. Eschatologically, the promise points beyond the post-exilic return to the millennial restoration of Israel.
1Pet.5.10: After ye have suffered a while — suffering is brief; the glory is eternal. Four divine actions: perfect (katartisei, complete), stablish (stērixei), strengthen (sthenōsei), settle (themeliōsei, set on a foundation). The God of all grace finishes what suffering refines.
John.16.33: In the world ye shall have tribulation — the present pluralism of trials. But be of good cheer (tharseite) — be courageous — I have overcome (nenikēka, perfect tense — abiding result) the world. The believer's peace is rooted in Christ's accomplished victory.
Isa.9.6: The Messianic child has four throne-names: Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God (El gibbor — incontestably divine), Everlasting Father (Father of eternity), Prince of Peace. The verse is a Christological mountain peak in the OT.
John.14.15: If ye love me — present conditional, ongoing love — keep (tēreō, watch over) my commandments. Love is the soil; obedience is the fruit. Antinomianism inverts this verse; legalism severs it.
Jas.1.12: Blessed (makarios) is the man that endureth (hypomenei) temptation — the same word translated 'blessed' in the Beatitudes. The crown of life is for them that love Him — not as merit but as covenantal token of approved faith.
Gal.2.20: Crucified (synestaurōmai, perfect tense — past act with abiding effect) with Christ. The believer's old self is over; what now lives is Christ in him, accessed by faith in the Son of God who loved me and gave Himself for me. Personal substitution becomes personal possession.
Rom.12.1: Therefore (oun) — drawing the practical inference from chapters 1–11. By the mercies of God — chapter 12 onward is the response to mercy. Present (parastēsai) your bodies — the OT priestly verb — as a living sacrifice. This is your reasonable (logikēn, rational) service.
John.14.27: My peace I give unto you — Christ's peace, possessive. Not as the world giveth — the world's peace is circumstantial; Christ's is positional. Let not your heart be troubled, neither let it be afraid — twin imperatives grounded in the gift.
Josh.1.8: Three commands for the new leader: this book of the law shall not depart from thy mouth (recite it), thou shalt meditate (haggāh, ruminate) therein day and night, observe to do all that is written. The promise: prosperous and good success — covenantally, not pragmatically.
Isa.26.3: Perfect peace (shalom shalom, doubled for emphasis) is granted to the one whose mind is stayed (samuk, leaning) on God, because he trusts. The condition for unshakeable peace is unbroken trust.
Ps.51.10: David's penitential cry after Bathsheba: create (bara' — same verb as Genesis 1:1) in me a clean heart. Only God can create ex nihilo — the request acknowledges that moral renewal is supernatural. Renew a right (nakon, steadfast) spirit within me.
Ps.91.1: He that dwelleth (yashav, sits down) in the secret place of the Most High (Elyon) shall abide (lun, lodge) under the shadow of the Almighty (Shaddai). Four divine names cluster in two verses to assure the worshiper.
Jer.15.16: Thy words were found, and I did eat them — the prophet ingested the Word. The result: the joy and rejoicing of mine heart. Bibliophagy as the prophet's spiritual diet.
Mark.16.16: He that believeth and is baptized shall be saved — faith is the instrument; baptism is the outward profession. He that believeth not shall be damned — note: damnation is grounded in unbelief, not lack of baptism. Salvation is conditioned on faith alone.
1Pet.5.8: Be sober (nēpsate, alert and self-controlled), be vigilant (grēgorēsate, awake) — twin aorist imperatives. The adversary, the devil, walks about (peripatei) as a roaring lion seeking whom he may devour. Christian sobriety is spiritual warfare.
Heb.4.12: The Word of God is living (zōn) and active (energēs), sharper than any two-edged sword. Pierces (diiknoumenos) — penetrates — to division of soul and spirit. Discerns (kritikos) the thoughts and intents of the heart. Scripture is no dead letter; it is God's surgical instrument.
Eph.1.4: He hath chosen (exelexato) us in Him before the foundation of the world. Election is pre-temporal, in Christ, and unto holiness — that we should be holy and without blame before Him in love. Predestination is to character, not just to status.
John.1.14: And the Word was made flesh (sarx egeneto) — the incarnation in five Greek words. Dwelt (eskēnōsen, tabernacled) among us. We beheld His glory — apostolic eyewitness, not legend. Full of grace and truth — the Old Testament hesed and emet incarnated.
2Tim.2.19: Nevertheless the foundation of God standeth sure (estēken — perfect tense). It bears two seals: the LORD knoweth them that are His (election), and Let every one that nameth the name of Christ depart from iniquity (sanctification). Divine sovereignty and human responsibility, side by side.
Matt.9.13: Jesus quotes Hosea 6:6: I will have mercy and not sacrifice. Then He locates His mission: I am not come to call the righteous (those who think they are) but sinners to repentance. The gospel is for the self-aware unworthy.
2Chr.7.14: If my people, called by my name (covenant identity), shall humble themselves, pray, seek my face, turn from their wicked ways — four conditions, then I will hear, forgive, heal. The promise is to OT Israel under the Mosaic covenant; the principle of repentant prayer remains paradigmatic.
Ps.84.11: The LORD God is a sun (warmth and light) and shield (protection); He gives grace and glory. No good thing will He withhold from them that walk uprightly. The promise is conditioned on the walk, not on circumstances.
Mic.6.8: He hath shewed thee, O man, what is good — divine revelation, not human guessing. What doth the LORD require of thee — covenantal demand. Three mandates: do justly, love mercy (chesed), walk humbly with thy God. The summary of OT ethics in one verse.
John.8.12: I am the light of the world — the second I AM saying. He that followeth (akolouthōn, present participle of continual following) shall not walk in darkness but have the light of life. To follow Christ is to live in His light.
Ps.33.10: The LORD bringeth the counsel of the heathen to nought; He maketh the devices of the people of none effect. Divine sovereignty over the geopolitical chessboard. The verse should produce calm in believers reading the news.
Ps.55.22: Cast (shalakh, throw decisively) thy burden upon the LORD, and He shall sustain thee. He shall never suffer the righteous to be moved (mot — slip permanently). Burdens are not denied; they are transferred.
Heb.13.5: Let your conversation (manner of life) be without covetousness; be content with such things as ye have. The reason: He hath said, I will never (ou mē — strongest Greek negation) leave thee, nor (oude mē) forsake thee. Five negatives in the Greek for emphasis.
Ps.37.4: Delight thyself (hith'annag) in the LORD — luxuriate in Him. The result: He shall give thee the desires of thine heart — meaning, when God is your delight, your desires are reshaped to align with His will.
Phil.4.19: My God shall supply (plērōsei, fill up) all your need according to His riches in glory by Christ Jesus. The supply is according to (kata) — proportionate to — God's riches, not merely out of them. The Philippians' generosity (vv. 14–18) sets the context.
Jer.1.5: Before I formed thee in the belly I knew thee — divine election prior to conception. Sanctified (qadash, set apart) thee — pre-natal consecration. The verse undergirds both the prophet's calling and the dignity of unborn human life.
Heb.12.1: The cloud (nephos) of witnesses — the heroes of chapter 11. Lay aside every weight (onkos) and the sin which doth so easily beset (euperistaton). Run with patience (hypomonēs, endurance) the race set before us. The athletic metaphor demands stripping down and pacing through pain.
Joel.2.28: I will pour out my Spirit upon all flesh — the OT promise of universal Spirit-effusion. Peter cites this at Pentecost (Acts 2:17–21) as inaugurated; the consummation awaits the eschaton. The Spirit's outpouring is no longer restricted by tribe, gender, or age.
Isa.48.17: Thy Redeemer (go'el — kinsman-redeemer), the Holy One of Israel — covenant titles. I am the LORD thy God which teacheth thee to profit (ya'al, benefit), which leadeth thee by the way thou shouldest go. Divine pedagogy is purposive.
Isa.44.18: Idolaters have not known (yada') nor understood — the heart is shut (tach, smeared over) so that they cannot see. Idolatry is a noetic problem before it is an ethical one; corrected hearts perceive God rightly.
Matt.11.28: Come unto me — the great gospel imperative. All ye that labor (kopiōntes, work to exhaustion) and are heavy laden (pephortismenoi, perfect tense — already loaded down). I will give you rest (anapausō) — sovereign gift, not negotiated.
Heb.11.6: Without faith it is impossible to please Him — categorical exclusion. The believer must come to God believing He is, and that He is a rewarder of them that diligently seek Him. Bare existence-belief plus seeking-faith.
1John.5.14: This is the confidence (parrēsia, frank boldness) we have toward Him: if we ask anything according to His will, He heareth us. Effective prayer is will-aligned, not appetite-driven.
Rom.5.8: God commendeth (synistēsin, demonstrates and proves) His love toward us — measurable, not merely declared. While we were yet sinners (still hostile, vv. 6, 10), Christ died for us. The timing of the cross magnifies the love.
Luke.4.18: Jesus reads Isa 61:1–2 in the Nazareth synagogue and applies it to Himself. The Spirit of the Lord is upon me — Trinitarian commissioning. To preach the gospel to the poor — His mission inverts every human valuation.
Zech.13.9: The remnant comes through the fire — refining is the means of covenant identification. They shall call on my name, and I will hear them. Eschatologically, this is the converted Jewish remnant of the tribulation.
Prov.29.23: A man's pride (ga'avah) shall bring him low — pride contains the seed of its own humiliation. Honour shall uphold the humble (shafal-ruach, lowly of spirit) — the moral economy of the kingdom inverts worldly logic.
1Cor.10.13: There hath no temptation taken you but such as is common (anthrōpinos, human-scaled) to man. God is faithful (pistos) — the controlling fact. He will not suffer you to be tempted above what ye are able, but will make a way to escape (ekbasin) — divine engineering of every trial.
Jer.31.33: This shall be the covenant: I will put my law in their inward parts and write it in their hearts. Heb 8:10–12 cites this as the new covenant inaugurated by Christ. The Mosaic covenant wrote on stone; the new writes on the heart by the Spirit.
Heb.12.2: Looking unto (aphorōntes — looking away from everything else to fix on) Jesus, the author (archēgos) and finisher (teleiōtēs) of our faith. The race is run with eyes on Him, not on circumstances or self.
Deut.4.9: Take heed (shamar) and keep thy soul diligently — guard your inner life. Lest thou forget the things which thine eyes have seen — covenant memory is a covenantal duty. Teach them to thy sons and thy sons' sons — generational transmission of revelation.
John.10.28: I give unto them eternal life — present indicative, sovereign giving. They shall never (ou mē, double negative) perish, neither shall any (oudeis) pluck them out of my hand. The doctrine of perseverance grounded in Christ's grip.
Eph.2.8: By grace are ye saved (sesōsmenoi — perfect periphrastic — completed action with abiding result), through faith. Not of yourselves (nor of works, v. 9) — every theory of synergistic salvation is excluded. It is the gift of God — every link of salvation is grace.
Jas.5.16: Confess your faults one to another, and pray one for another, that ye may be healed. The effectual fervent (energoumenē, in-working) prayer of a righteous man availeth much. Holiness is the conduit of prayer's power.
John.15.16: Ye have not chosen me, but I have chosen you — the priority of divine election. Ordained (ethēka) you to bring forth fruit, and that your fruit should remain. Election is to fruit-bearing, not to leisure.
Col.3.16: Let the word of Christ dwell (enoikeitō, take up residence) in you richly. Teaching and admonishing one another — corporate, not just private. Singing — psalms (OT psalms), hymns (Christian compositions), spiritual songs (Spirit-prompted) — with grace in your hearts to the Lord.
Phil.2.13: It is God which worketh (energōn) in you both to will (thelein) and to do (energein) of His good pleasure (eudokia). Sanctification is divinely sourced even in its volitional dimension. The believer wills and works — but God is the ultimate cause behind both.
John.11.25: I am the resurrection and the life — the fifth I AM saying, spoken at Lazarus's tomb. He that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live — physical death does not interrupt eternal life. The promise is grounded in Christ's identity, not just His ability.
Ps.31.19: Oh how great is thy goodness (tov), which thou hast laid up (tsafan, treasured up) for them that fear thee. Reserved goodness — the believer's portion is in escrow, set apart, awaiting full distribution.
Jas.1.17: Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of lights. With whom is no variableness (parallage) neither shadow of turning (tropēs aposkiasma) — astronomical metaphor for absolute immutability.
2Tim.1.9: Who hath saved us, and called us with a holy calling — past actions establishing present identity. Not according to our works, but according to His own purpose (prothesis) and grace, given us in Christ Jesus before the world began. Pre-temporal grace is the ground.
1Cor.2.9: Eye hath not seen, nor ear heard — Paul cites Isa 64:4 to show the things that God hath prepared for them that love Him are inaccessible to natural perception. Verse 10: God hath revealed them unto us by His Spirit. Revelation supplies what reason cannot reach.
Deut.31.6: Be strong (chazaq) and of a good courage (amats), fear not, nor be afraid — four imperatives. The reason: the LORD thy God, He it is that doth go with thee; He will not fail thee, nor forsake thee. Quoted in Heb 13:5 as still in force.
2Tim.3.16: All scripture (pasa graphē) is given by inspiration of God (theopneustos — God-breathed). Profitable for doctrine (teaching), reproof (rebuke), correction (restoration), instruction in righteousness — every aspect of pastoral ministry. The verse anchors the doctrine of inerrancy and sufficiency.
Ezek.36.26: A new heart will I give you, and a new spirit will I put within you. The stony heart (lev ha'even) is replaced with a heart of flesh — divine surgery, not human improvement. Regeneration in covenantal terms.
Rom.8.1: There is therefore now no condemnation (katakrima) to them which are in Christ Jesus. Now (nyn) — present, abiding state. In Christ Jesus — the locus. Justification's verdict is permanent and personal.
Matt.5.3: Blessed (makarios) are the poor (ptōchoi, beggarly) in spirit — those who recognize their spiritual bankruptcy. For theirs is the kingdom of heaven — present tense. Spiritual poverty is the entry condition; pride disqualifies.
Jer.32.17: Ah Lord GOD! behold, thou hast made the heaven and the earth by thy great power and stretched out arm — creation as the proof of omnipotence. There is nothing too hard (pala', wonderful, impossible) for thee. Argument from creation to providential capacity.
1John.2.15: Love not (mē agapate, present imperative — stop loving) the world, neither the things in the world. The cosmos here is the fallen system organized against God. If any man love the world, the love of the Father is not in him — exclusive loves.
Ps.27.14: Wait (qavah — actively expect) on the LORD: be of good courage. He shall strengthen thine heart — divine reinforcement of inner resolve. The imperative is repeated, like a teacher pressing the lesson.
2Cor.5.21: He hath made Him to be sin (hamartian) for us, who knew no sin — the great exchange. That we might be made the righteousness of God in Him. Imputation runs both directions: our sin to Him, His righteousness to us. The doctrine of justification compressed.
1Pet.2.24: Who His own self bare our sins in His own body on the tree — penal substitution stated baldly. By whose stripes (mōlōpi — bruise) ye were healed (iathēte, aorist passive — completed). The 'healing' here is forgiveness of sins; physical healing is at best a derivative application.
Heb.11.1: Faith is the substance (hypostasis, foundational reality) of things hoped for, the evidence (elenchos, conviction) of things not seen. Faith treats God's promises as more solid than visible circumstance.
Prov.3.6: In all thy ways acknowledge Him — yada', know Him intimately as Lord over every department of life. He shall direct (yashar, make straight) thy paths. Submission produces guidance; the verse is not a magic formula but a covenantal promise.
Mark.8.18: Having eyes, see ye not? — Jesus rebukes apostolic dullness about the loaves. Spiritual perception is not automatic; even the disciples must be trained. The same charge runs through Isaiah 6:9–10.
Mark.11.24: What things soever ye desire, when ye pray, believe that ye receive them, and ye shall have them. Faith is the present-tense possession of the not-yet. The verse must be read with v. 25 (forgiveness as condition) and v. 22 (faith in God, not faith in faith).
1John.4.4: Greater is He that is in you, than he that is in the world. The believer's confidence in spiritual conflict is ontological: the indwelling Christ outranks the indwelling spirit of antichrist in unbelievers.
Phil.4.8: Six categories — true, honest, just, pure, lovely, of good report — and a summary clause: virtue and praise. Think on (logizesthe, calculate, dwell on) these things. Mental discipline is a Christian duty.
Isa.43.1: Fear not — the imperative grounded in three covenantal claims: I have redeemed (ga'al) thee, I have called thee by thy name, thou art mine. Personal possession is the antidote to fear.
Acts.1.8: Ye shall receive power (dynamis) — divine enablement — when the Holy Ghost is come upon you. The geographic outline (Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, ends of the earth) structures the rest of Acts. The mission is empowered, not improvised.
Phil.4.7: The peace of God, which passeth all understanding — exceeding human cognitive grasp. Shall keep (phrourēsei, military verb — garrison) your hearts and minds through Christ Jesus. Peace as a sentry posted at the gates of inner life.
Jas.1.5: If any of you lack wisdom (sophia, practical know-how for trials) — let him ask of God. He giveth to all men liberally (haplōs, single-mindedly), and upbraideth not. Divine generosity has no resentment.
Isa.54.17: No weapon formed against thee shall prosper — covenantal protection promised to the servants of the LORD. This is their heritage — inheritance language. The verse is corporate to Israel before it is individual to the saint.
Col.3.1: If ye then be risen with Christ — first-class conditional, treating it as fact — seek (zēteite, present imperative) those things which are above. The risen life is heavenward in orientation.
Matt.28.19: Go ye therefore (poreuthentes — having gone) and make disciples (mathēteusate — the only main verb) of all nations. Baptizing and teaching are the two participles describing the disciple-making process. Trinitarian baptismal formula confirms the deity of all three Persons.
Eph.3.16: That He would grant you, according to the riches of His glory (the supply), to be strengthened with might (dynamei) by His Spirit (the agency) in the inner man (esō anthrōpon — the regenerate self). Sanctification is interior strengthening.
Ps.37.5: Commit (galal — roll over) thy way unto the LORD; trust also in Him. He shall bring it to pass — divine engineering of human surrender. The verb-pictures are vivid: roll your burden, lean on Him, watch Him work.
Eph.4.22: Put off (apothesthai, like discarding old clothes) concerning the former conversation the old man, which is corrupt according to the deceitful lusts. The change-of-clothes metaphor runs through vv. 22–24. Sanctification is dramatic personal exchange.
Jer.29.12: Then shall ye call upon me, and ye shall go and pray unto me, and I will hearken unto you. The promise comes after seventy years of exile (v. 10) — God's silence has a terminus.
Eph.6.18: Praying always (en panti kairō — in every season) with all prayer and supplication in the Spirit. Watching thereunto with all perseverance and supplication for all saints. Prayer is the atmosphere of the armored Christian.
Jas.3.17: The wisdom that is from above is first pure (hagnē), then peaceable, gentle, easy to be entreated, full of mercy and good fruits, without partiality, and without hypocrisy. Eight marks of heavenly wisdom contrasted with earthly demonic wisdom (v. 15).
John.7.37: In the last day, that great day of the feast — the climax of Tabernacles, when water was poured out — Jesus stood and cried: If any man thirst, let him come unto me, and drink. The risen-Christ-as-living-water imagery dominates John.
Zeph.3.19: I will deal with all that afflict thee — eschatological vindication. I will save her that halteth (the lame) and gather her that was driven out — the marginalized restored. God's restoration of His people is not theoretical.
Acts.2.38: Repent (metanoēsate, change of mind) and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the remission (eis aphesin) of sins. The eis is debated, but consistent NT teaching is that baptism testifies to a remission already received by faith. Then receive the gift of the Holy Spirit.
Isa.30.21: Thine ears shall hear a word behind thee — divine guidance from beyond. This is the way, walk ye in it — auditory prompt, conscience-applied. The promise is post-discipline (v. 20).
Rom.12.12: Three present participles — rejoicing in hope, patient in tribulation, continuing instant in prayer. Three Christian disciplines, knit together as one practice.
Acts.26.18: Paul recounts his commission: to open their eyes, turn them from darkness to light, from the power of Satan unto God, that they may receive forgiveness and inheritance among them which are sanctified. Conversion as cosmic transfer of allegiance.
Col.3.17: Whatsoever ye do in word or deed, do all in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God and the Father by Him. Christian totalism — every word, every act, christologically ordered.
Isa.57.15: The high and lofty One — God's transcendence — that inhabiteth eternity, whose name is Holy, dwells in the high and holy place — and with the contrite (dakka', crushed) and humble. Transcendence and immanence joined.
Isa.43.2: When thou passest through the waters, I will be with thee — not promise of exemption but companionship. Through fires unburnt, through floods undrowned. Israel's typological history (Red Sea, Jordan) becomes the pattern for every saint.
1John.4.9: In this was manifested the love of God toward us, because that God sent His only begotten (monogenē) Son into the world, that we might live through Him. Love is defined christologically — by what God did at the cross, not by sentiment.
John.14.21: He that hath my commandments, and keepeth them, he it is that loveth me. The verse defines love by obedience, not feeling. He that loveth me shall be loved of my Father, and I will love him, and will manifest myself to him — a deepening triangulation of love.
Prov.16.3: Commit (galal, roll) thy works unto the LORD, and thy thoughts shall be established. Surrender precedes stability. Mental clarity is the fruit of consecration, not its pre-condition.
Ps.46.1: God is our refuge (machseh) and strength, a very present (m'od nimtsa', much-found) help in trouble. Three covenantal claims compressed into one verse. The anchor for vv. 2–3.
Isa.40.29: He giveth power to the faint; and to them that have no might He increaseth strength. The recipients are not the strong but the weak; supply matches need.
Ezek.36.25: I will sprinkle clean water upon you — ritual cleansing pictures spiritual reality. From all your filthiness and from all your idols will I cleanse you. The new covenant cleanses both moral pollution and idolatrous attachment.
2Cor.4.4: The god of this world (aiōn — age, system) hath blinded the minds of them which believe not. Spiritual blindness is satanic; conversion requires divine intervention to lift the veil (cf. v. 6).
Matt.6.25: Take no thought (mē merimnate, present imperative — stop being anxious) for your life. Verses 26–32 ground the prohibition in providential observation: birds, lilies, and the Father's knowledge of your need.
Matt.5.6: Blessed are they which do hunger and thirst (peinōntes kai dipsōntes — present participles of intense desire) after righteousness, for they shall be filled (chortasthēsontai — divine passive). Righteousness-hunger is rewarded with divine satisfaction.
Jer.17.7: Blessed is the man that trusteth (batach) in the LORD, and whose hope (mivtach, security) the LORD is. The contrast with the cursed man (v. 5) who trusts in flesh defines two ways of life.
Zech.4.6: Not by might (chayil) nor by power (koach) but by my Spirit (ruach), saith the LORD of hosts. The post-exilic temple is built by divine pneumatology, not human resourcing. Principle for every God-ordained work.
Isa.12.2: Behold, God is my salvation; I will trust, and not be afraid. The LORD JEHOVAH is my strength and my song; He also is become my salvation. Echoes Exod 15:2 — the Song of Moses now sung in eschatological reality.
Isa.55.8: My thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways my ways, saith the LORD. The qualitative gap between divine and human reasoning is irreducible. The verse rebukes presumption and grounds humility.
Jas.1.19: Be swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath. Three asymmetric speeds for the Christian. The verse is the antidote to Twitter.
John.14.26: The Comforter (paraklētos), which is the Holy Ghost, whom the Father will send in my name — Trinitarian sending. He shall teach you all things and bring all things to your remembrance — the apostolic guarantee behind the New Testament.
Jas.4.8: Draw nigh to God, and He will draw nigh to you. Cleanse your hands, ye sinners, and purify your hearts, ye double-minded (dipsychoi — two-souled). Repentance is the gateway to nearness.
Isa.60.1: Arise, shine; for thy light is come, and the glory of the LORD is risen upon thee. Eschatological summons to Zion. The light is divine, not generated by Israel.
Exod.34.6: The LORD passed by before him and proclaimed: The LORD, the LORD God, merciful and gracious, longsuffering, abundant in goodness and truth (chesed and emet). The most-quoted self-revelation of God in the OT.
Num.23.19: God is not a man, that He should lie; nor the son of man, that He should repent. Hath He said, and shall He not do it? The doctrine of divine truthfulness in propositional form.
Col.3.12: Put on (endysasthe — clothe yourselves) as the elect of God, holy and beloved, bowels of mercies (compassion), kindness, humbleness of mind, meekness, longsuffering. Five virtues that constitute Christian wardrobe.
Matt.28.18: All authority (exousia) is given unto me in heaven and in earth. The post-resurrection assertion grounds the Great Commission (v. 19). Christ's universal lordship is the engine of mission.
Matt.11.27: All things are delivered unto me of my Father — Christ's mediatorial possession of all reality. No man knoweth the Son but the Father; neither knoweth any man the Father, save the Son, and he to whomsoever the Son will reveal Him. The exclusivity of revelation through Christ alone.
John.1.12: As many as received Him, to them gave He power (exousia, right) to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on His name. The right to sonship is granted on the condition of faith.
Jer.29.13: Ye shall seek me, and find me, when ye shall search for me with all your heart. Half-hearted seeking does not find Him; the condition is total. The promise is universally true beyond its first historical setting.
Eph.4.29: Let no corrupt (sapros, rotten) communication proceed out of your mouth, but that which is good to the use of edifying (oikodomē, building up). Speech is either constructive or corrosive; there is no neutral.
John.10.10: The thief cometh not but for to steal, and to kill, and to destroy — Satan's three-fold malice. I am come that they might have life, and that they might have it more abundantly (perissos, super-abundantly). The contrast is total.
Matt.7.21: Not every one that saith unto me, Lord, Lord, shall enter into the kingdom of heaven. He that doeth the will of my Father — saving faith manifests in obedience. Verbal profession without obedience is empty.
John.15.5: I am the vine; ye are the branches. Apart from me ye can do nothing — categorical. Spiritual fruit is impossible apart from union with Christ; this rules out moralism in any form.
Isa.44.2: Thus saith the LORD that made thee, and formed thee from the womb. Fear not, O Jacob, my servant; and thou, Jeshurun (the upright one), whom I have chosen. The covenant maker speaks personally to His chosen.
1Cor.8.3: If any man love God, the same is known of Him. The order in salvation is divine knowledge first; human love responds. To be known of God is the deeper privilege.
Ps.145.18: The LORD is nigh (qarov) unto all them that call upon Him, to all that call upon Him in truth (emet). Sincerity is the qualifier; the proximity is universal in offer.
Matt.5.16: Let your light so shine before men that they may see your good works, and glorify your Father which is in heaven. Visibility serves doxology; the goal is not your reputation but the Father's glory.
John.3.36: He that believeth on the Son hath (echei, present tense) everlasting life. He that believeth not the Son shall not see life — the wrath of God abideth (menei, remains) on him. Salvation and judgment are present-tense realities determined by faith.
Exod.14.14: The LORD shall fight for you, and ye shall hold your peace. Israel's role at the Red Sea was passive faith. The verse is template for divine deliverance.
John.10.27: My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me. Three present-tense markers of saving relationship. Election produces these realities; they do not produce election.
Eccl.11.6: In the morning sow thy seed, and in the evening withhold not thine hand. Diligence in uncertain seasons. The wisdom of investing breadth-wise when outcomes are obscure.
2Cor.9.8: God is able (dynatos) to make all grace abound (perisseusai) toward you. That ye, having all sufficiency in all things, may abound to every good work. Generous saints are super-supplied for super-generosity.
Titus.2.14: Who gave Himself for us, that He might redeem (lytrōsētai, ransom) us from all iniquity, and purify (katharisē) unto Himself a peculiar (periousion, specially possessed) people, zealous of good works. Particular redemption stated explicitly.
John.4.14: Whosoever drinketh of the water that I shall give him shall never thirst — the well within. The water shall be in him a well of water springing up into everlasting life. Eternal life is internalized fountain, not external supply.
Ps.23.4: Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death (tsalmavet — deep darkness), I will fear no evil — the negation. For thou art with me — the cause of fearlessness. The shepherd's rod (defense) and staff (correction) comfort.
Mic.7.18: Who is a God like unto thee, that pardoneth iniquity, and passeth by the transgression of the remnant? He retaineth not His anger forever, because He delighteth in mercy. The Hebrew name Micah ('Who is like Yahweh?') becomes the verse's theme.
Luke.18.1: He spake a parable unto them to this end, that men ought always to pray and not to faint (enkakein, lose heart). Persistent prayer is the antidote to discouragement.
Acts.4.12: Neither is there salvation in any other; for there is none other name under heaven given among men, whereby we must be saved. Two universal negatives in one verse. Religious pluralism is excluded.
Rev.13.8: All that dwell upon the earth shall worship him (the beast), whose names are not written in the book of life of the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world. The book of life is pre-temporally written; the Lamb's slaying is in the eternal counsel of God.
2Cor.3.18: We all, with open face beholding (katoptrizomenoi) as in a glass the glory of the Lord, are changed (metamorphoumetha — present passive) into the same image from glory to glory. Sanctification is incremental conformation through beholding.
1John.5.20: The Son of God is come, and hath given us an understanding (dianoia) that we may know Him that is true. This is the true God and eternal life — Christ designated outright as God.
Lam.3.25: The LORD is good unto them that wait for Him, to the soul that seeketh Him. In the rubble of Jerusalem's destruction, Jeremiah affirms divine goodness toward seekers. The verse is hope amidst ash.
Isa.44.6: I am the first, and I am the last; and beside me there is no God. The most explicit OT statement of monotheism, applied in Rev 1:17 and 22:13 to Christ — proving His full deity.
1John.1.7: If we walk in the light, as He is in the light, we have fellowship one with another, and the blood of Jesus Christ His Son cleanseth (katharizei, present tense, ongoing) us from all sin. Habitual fellowship rests on continuous cleansing.
Luke.19.10: The Son of man is come to seek and to save that which was lost. Christ's mission summarized in one verse. Seeking precedes saving; no one finds Christ who was not first sought by Christ.
Ps.32.8: I will instruct thee and teach thee in the way which thou shalt go; I will guide thee with mine eye. The Father's pedagogy of His covenant child. The eye-guidance is intimate, not coercive.
Rom.15.13: The God of hope (elpis) fill (plērōsai) you with all joy and peace in believing, that ye may abound in hope through the power of the Holy Ghost. Trinitarian benediction. Hope is both source and fruit.
Jas.1.2: My brethren, count it all joy when ye fall into divers temptations (peirasmois — trials). The reckoning is volitional, not emotional. Joy in trial is theological discipline, not natural temperament.
Rev.1.5: Jesus Christ, the faithful witness, the first begotten (prōtotokos) of the dead, the prince (archōn) of the kings of the earth. Threefold Christological title. Unto Him that loved us, and washed (lousanti) us from our sins in His own blood.
Eph.1.17: That the God of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of glory, may give unto you the Spirit of wisdom and revelation in the knowledge (epignōsis, full knowledge) of Him. Trinitarian prayer for relational illumination.
Rom.8.32: He that spared not (ouk epheisato) His own Son but delivered Him up for us all — the cross is the irrefutable demonstration. The argument is from the greater to the lesser: how shall He not also freely give us all things? If God gave the Son, every lesser provision is guaranteed.
Ps.46.10: Be still (harpu — let go, cease striving) and know that I am God. The imperative is not contemplative inaction but cessation of self-effort in the presence of divine sovereignty. The verse is military first (cease the war), only then meditative.
Eph.4.32: Be ye kind (chrēstoi), tenderhearted, forgiving one another. The standard: even as God for Christ's sake hath forgiven you. Christian forgiveness is patterned on and powered by the cross.
Isa.26.20: Come, my people, enter thou into thy chambers — eschatological refuge during the day of indignation. The pattern echoes the Passover: hide until the wrath passes. Applied throughout history when judgment falls.
Gal.5.1: Stand fast in the liberty wherewith Christ hath made us free — perfect tense, the freedom is accomplished. Be not entangled again with the yoke of bondage — the Judaizers' return to law-keeping is a return to slavery. Liberty is positional first, ethical second.
Ps.50.15: Call upon me in the day of trouble: I will deliver thee, and thou shalt glorify me. Three movements: prayer, deliverance, doxology. The transaction has God's glory as its terminus.
Luke.1.37: With God nothing shall be impossible (ou adynatēsei para tou theou pan rhēma) — Gabriel's word to Mary. The Greek literally: 'no word from God shall be without power.' Divine speech equals divine performance.
1Sam.7.3: Samuel's revival call: if ye do return unto the LORD with all your hearts, then put away the strange gods. Repentance is not feeling but discarding — putting idols away. He will deliver follows the obedience.
John.14.13: Whatsoever ye shall ask in my name, that will I do — prayer in His name is prayer aligned with His character and mission. The purpose: that the Father may be glorified in the Son. Answered prayer is doxological theater.
Eph.6.12: We wrestle (palē — close-quarter combat) not against flesh and blood, but against principalities, powers, rulers of darkness, spiritual wickedness in high places. Four ranks of demonic opposition. Christian conflict is supernaturally located.
Col.1.13: He hath delivered (errysato — rescued) us from the power (exousia, authority) of darkness, and translated (metestēsen — transferred) us into the kingdom of His dear Son. Conversion is regime change, not self-improvement.
Luke.11.28: Yea rather, blessed are they that hear the word of God, and keep it. Jesus redirects a woman's blessing of Mary's biology to the deeper blessedness of obedient hearing. Privilege of relation is less than privilege of obedience.
Gen.15.1: Fear not, Abram: I am thy shield (magen) and thy exceeding great reward. Abram's reward is not the gift but the Giver. Personal possession of God is the true inheritance.
John.8.32: Ye shall know the truth, and the truth shall make you free. Verse 31 conditions this on continuing in Christ's word. Doctrinal truth, sustained by abiding, produces real liberation — not Enlightenment notions of freedom.
John.1.29: Behold the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world! John the Baptist identifies the Passover Lamb (Exod 12) and Isaiah's Suffering Servant (Isa 53). The lamb-typology runs from Genesis to Revelation.
2Cor.6.14: Be ye not unequally yoked (heterozygountes) with unbelievers. The agricultural image (Deut 22:10): two animals of different strength cannot pull straight. The application is comprehensive — marriage, business, ministry partnership.
Matt.21.22: All things, whatsoever ye shall ask in prayer, believing, ye shall receive. Faith is the channel; the asking must be will-aligned (1 John 5:14). Not a blank check — but no ceiling on what God may grant the praying believer.
1Cor.6.9: The unrighteous shall not inherit the kingdom of God. Be not deceived — Paul's strong warning. The list (vv. 9–10) excludes those whose lives habitually pattern these sins. Verse 11 follows: 'and such were some of you' — the gospel transforms.
Matt.28.20: Teaching them to observe (tērein — guard, keep) all things whatsoever I have commanded you. Discipleship is comprehensive obedience, not selective hearing. Lo, I am with you alway, even unto the end of the world (synteleias tou aiōnos) — the great promise underwriting the great commission.
Luke.10.19: I give unto you power to tread on serpents and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy. The disciples' authority is delegated, not autonomous. The greater joy (v. 20) is that their names are written in heaven.
John.7.38: Out of his belly shall flow rivers of living water. Verse 39 identifies this as the Spirit, given after Christ's glorification. Pentecost will inaugurate this; the indwelt believer becomes the channel of divine refreshment.
John.4.10: If thou knewest the gift of God, and who it is — the conversation pivots on identification. Christ Himself is the living water; recognizing Him is the first step in receiving Him.
Eph.1.7: In whom we have redemption (apolytrōsis — release by ransom) through His blood, the forgiveness of sins, according to the riches of His grace. Redemption is past-completed; the riches measure its supply.
Titus.3.5: Not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to His mercy He saved us, by the washing of regeneration (palingenesia) and renewing of the Holy Ghost. Salvation's ground (mercy), means (regeneration), and agent (Spirit) all stated.
1Cor.3.16: Know ye not that ye (plural) are the temple (naos) of God, and the Spirit of God dwelleth in you? The local church corporately is God's temple; defiling it (vv. 17) brings divine destruction.
Mal.3.6: I am the LORD, I change not — divine immutability. Therefore ye sons of Jacob are not consumed — Israel's preservation rests on God's unchanging covenant fidelity, not their performance.
Isa.55.7: Let the wicked forsake his way, and the unrighteous man his thoughts. Repentance is twofold: behavior and mind. Return unto the LORD, and He will have mercy; abundantly pardon.
Heb.7.25: He is able to save them to the uttermost (eis to panteles — to the very end, completely) that come unto God by Him, seeing He ever liveth to make intercession. Christ's permanent priesthood guarantees permanent salvation.
Isa.43.19: Behold, I will do a new thing — divine innovation. The wilderness becomes a road; the desert becomes rivers. Eschatological promise of restoration, with NT typological extension to the new creation in Christ.
Ps.25.3: Let none that wait on thee be ashamed — the prayer for vindication. The doctrine: those whose hope is in the LORD will not be put to shame, in time or eternity. Romans 10:11 echoes this.
Isa.46.10: Declaring the end from the beginning, and from ancient times the things that are not yet done. Divine omniscience extends to the future and divine sovereignty extends to bringing it to pass. My counsel shall stand — comprehensive teleology.
Col.1.11: Strengthened with all might (dynamei) according to His glorious power (kratos), unto all patience (hypomonēn) and longsuffering with joyfulness. Divine power produces uncomplaining endurance.
1Pet.2.5: Ye also, as lively stones, are built up a spiritual house, an holy priesthood. Two metaphors fused: building and priesthood. The believer is both the temple's stone and its priest.
1Pet.1.22: Seeing ye have purified your souls in obeying the truth — the new birth produces moral transformation. Love one another with a pure heart fervently (ektenōs — strenuously). Ethics flows from regeneration.
John.6.27: Labour not for the meat which perisheth, but for that meat which endureth unto everlasting life — Christ Himself (v. 35). The bread metaphor recurs throughout John 6.
Eph.6.11: Put on the whole armour (panoplia) of God, that ye may be able to stand against the wiles (methodeias — strategies) of the devil. Comprehensive divine outfitting against systematic opposition.
Eph.1.11: In whom also we have obtained an inheritance, being predestinated according to the purpose of Him who worketh all things after the counsel of His own will. Universal divine determinism stated in Pauline form. God's will runs through every event.
John.3.3: Except a man be born again (gennēthē anōthen — born from above), he cannot see the kingdom of God. Regeneration is prior to perception. Spiritual sight requires spiritual birth.
Col.1.15: Who is the image (eikōn) of the invisible God, the firstborn (prōtotokos) of every creature. Firstborn here means rank/preeminence (cf. Ps 89:27), not chronological origin. Christ is the visible manifestation of the unseen God.
2Cor.4.17: Our light affliction, which is but for a moment, worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight (baros) of glory. Affliction has eschatological productive value — it is the chisel of glory.
John.15.4: Abide in me, and I in you. As the branch cannot bear fruit of itself, except it abide in the vine — natural impossibility unless connected. Spiritual fruit requires unbroken union.
Isa.53.5: He was wounded for our transgressions, He was bruised for our iniquities. Penal substitution in the OT before the NT. The chastisement of our peace was upon Him; with His stripes we are healed.
Col.3.10: Have put on the new man, which is renewed (anakainoumenon — present passive) in knowledge after the image (eikōn) of Him that created him. Sanctification is image-restoration through ongoing renewal in knowledge.
Prov.20.22: Say not thou, I will recompense evil; but wait on the LORD, and He shall save thee. Vengeance is forfeited to God. Personal restraint is the pious response to wrong.
Exod.15.11: Who is like unto thee, O LORD, among the gods? — the Song of Moses' rhetorical question. Glorious in holiness, fearful in praises, doing wonders. Divine incomparability after the Red Sea.
Matt.19.26: With men this is impossible (the salvation of the rich, v. 25); but with God all things are possible. Salvation is divine work; human capacity is irrelevant. The verse generalizes well beyond the rich.
1Cor.1.30: Of Him are ye in Christ Jesus, who of God is made unto us wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, redemption. Four facets of Christ's saving work, all sourced in Christ Himself. Christ is not the gift-giver only; He is the gift.
2Cor.10.4: The weapons of our warfare are not carnal (sarkika — fleshly), but mighty through God to the pulling down of strongholds (ochyrōmatōn — fortresses). Spiritual warfare requires spiritual weaponry.
1Cor.13.4: Charity (agapē) suffereth long, and is kind. The famous love chapter opens with patience and kindness. Verbs throughout — love is what love does, not what love feels.
Luke.6.45: A good man out of the good treasure of his heart bringeth forth that which is good. The mouth merely reveals the heart's contents. Ethical speech is heart-diagnostic.
John.17.3: This is life eternal, that they might know thee the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom thou hast sent. Eternal life defined as relational knowledge, not duration. Trinitarian salvation — Father and Son named together.
2Cor.10.5: Casting down imaginations (logismous — reasonings) and every high thing that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God, bringing into captivity (aichmalōtizontes) every thought to the obedience of Christ. Mental warfare is the believer's daily discipline.
1Pet.3.18: Christ also hath once (hapax — once for all) suffered for sins, the just for the unjust, that He might bring us to God. Substitutionary atonement and reconciliation in one verse.
Heb.13.8: Jesus Christ the same yesterday, and today, and forever. Divine immutability applied to the Son. The verse anchors stability in a changing world.
Rev.5.9: Thou hast redeemed us to God by thy blood out of every kindred, and tongue, and people, and nation. The Lamb's blood is the ransom; the redeemed are gathered from every ethnic stratum. Particular redemption with global scope.
1Sam.16.7: Man looketh on the outward appearance, but the LORD looketh on the heart. Anointing of David rests on inward, not outward, qualification. Divine evaluation is interior.
John.13.34: A new commandment I give unto you, that ye love one another; as I have loved you. The newness is in the standard — Christ's self-giving love as the pattern. The commandment is old in form, new in measure.
Jas.1.22: Be ye doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving (paralogizomenoi — reasoning around) your own selves. Self-deception is the danger of bare hearing without acting.
Rom.5.1: Therefore being justified (dikaiōthentes — aorist passive, completed action) by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ. Justification is past; peace is present. The relational hostility ended at the cross.
1Pet.1.3: Begotten us again unto a lively hope by the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead. Regeneration produces living hope; Christ's resurrection is its ground. Past act, present hope, future inheritance.
Col.3.23: Whatsoever ye do, do it heartily (ek psychēs — from the soul), as to the Lord, and not unto men. Slaves' household work becomes worship when Christ-directed. The verse christianizes labor.
Gal.6.7: Be not deceived; God is not mocked (myktērizetai — sneered at). Whatsoever a man soweth, that shall he also reap. Spiritual cause-effect is invariable; God's moral order is not negotiable.
Ezek.11.19: I will give them one heart, and I will put a new spirit within you; I will take the stony heart out of their flesh and give them a heart of flesh. The new covenant prefigured. Regeneration is divine surgery.
Matt.7.24: Whosoever heareth these sayings of mine, and doeth them, I will liken him unto a wise man, which built his house upon a rock. Hearing alone is not building; obedience is the foundation. The Sermon on the Mount closes with this stark either-or.
John.5.24: He that heareth my word, and believeth on Him that sent me, hath everlasting life — present possession. Shall not come into condemnation; is passed from death unto life — perfect tense, abiding state. Eternal life is now.
Isa.55.11: My word that goeth forth out of my mouth shall not return unto me void, but it shall accomplish that which I please. Divine speech is performative — it does what it says. The Word is never inert.
Jer.17.9: The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately wicked: who can know it? Anthropology in one verse. Total depravity is not behaviorism (we always do worst) but corruption (every faculty tainted).
1Cor.13.13: Now abideth faith, hope, charity (agapē), these three; but the greatest of these is charity. Faith and hope are temporary (we walk by faith now, by sight then); love is eternal.
Eph.2.4: But God (ho theos — emphatic), who is rich in mercy, for His great love wherewith He loved us. The hinge of the chapter. Verses 1–3 describe deadness; 'But God' inaugurates resurrection.
Gen.18.14: Is anything too hard (yipale' — too wonderful, marvelous) for the LORD? The rhetorical question framing Sarah's pregnancy. Divine omnipotence in covenantal context.
Deut.6.5: Thou shalt love the LORD thy God with all thine heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy might. The Shema's second verse. Total-person love is the foundational covenant demand.
Mark.16.15: Go ye into all the world, and preach the gospel to every creature. Universal mandate. Mark's Great Commission emphasizes the breadth: every creature.
Prov.18.21: Death and life are in the power of the tongue. Words have lethal or life-giving force. The verse undergirds all biblical ethics of speech.
Ps.19.7: The law of the LORD is perfect (temimah — complete), converting the soul. The Word's effect on the soul is restorative — turning, refreshing, restoring. Six divine attributes of Scripture across vv. 7–9.
Col.1.10: That ye might walk worthy of the Lord unto all pleasing (areskeian) — daily conduct as doxology. Bearing fruit and increasing in the knowledge of God — the Christian life as fruitful learning.
Ps.118.6: The LORD is on my side; I will not fear: what can man do unto me? Cited in Heb 13:6. The fear of man is dispelled by the presence of God.
John.10.11: I am the good shepherd: the good shepherd giveth His life for the sheep. Particular atonement embedded in the shepherd metaphor. Christ dies for His sheep, by name (v. 14).
Acts.2.17: It shall come to pass in the last days, saith God, I will pour out of my Spirit upon all flesh. Peter cites Joel 2 to interpret Pentecost. The 'last days' begin at Christ's ascension and run to His return.
Rom.8.2: The law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus hath made me free from the law of sin and death. Two competing 'laws' — internal principles. The Spirit's life-giving power overcomes sin's death-bringing pull.
Ps.1.3: He shall be like a tree planted by the rivers of water, that bringeth forth his fruit in his season. The man who delights in God's law is permanently nourished, fruitful, and prosperous. Psalm 1 sets the bipolar moral universe of the Psalter.
Prov.18.10: The name of the LORD is a strong tower: the righteous runneth into it, and is safe. The covenant name as fortress. Faith is the running-into.
Ps.34.4: I sought the LORD, and He heard me, and delivered me from all my fears. Personal testimony grounding general principle. Seeking precedes finding.
1Sam.23.16: Jonathan went to David into the wood, and strengthened his hand in God. Friendship's highest service: pointing the discouraged to God. The model of Christian encouragement.
Matt.4.4: Man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of God. Jesus cites Deut 8:3 against the first temptation. Scripture is sustenance, not mere doctrine.
1John.3.1: Behold, what manner of love (potapēn agapēn — what foreign-country love) the Father hath bestowed upon us, that we should be called the sons of God! The love is alien — out of this world. Adoption is its measure.
Rev.7.17: The Lamb which is in the midst of the throne shall feed them, and shall lead them unto living fountains of waters. Eschatological pastoral imagery. The slaughtered Lamb is the eternal Shepherd.
Rom.14.17: The kingdom of God is not meat and drink — not external observance. But righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Ghost — Spirit-wrought spiritual realities. Kingdom essence vs. peripheral debate.
Heb.6.10: God is not unrighteous to forget your work and labour of love. Divine memory is moral; faithful service is permanently registered. Encouragement to weary saints.
1John.4.19: We love Him because He first loved us. Divine initiative is the source of all human Christian love. The order is irreversible.
Heb.1.3: Who being the brightness (apaugasma) of His glory, and the express image (charaktēr — exact stamp) of His person, and upholding all things by the word of His power. Christ's deity, distinction, and providential rule. The chapter's Christological mountain peak.
1Pet.5.5: Be clothed with humility (tapeinophrosynēn — encinctured, like an apron). God resisteth (antitassetai — sets Himself against) the proud, and giveth grace to the humble. Cited from Prov 3:34.
Eph.5.8: Ye were sometimes darkness, but now are ye light in the Lord. Walk as children of light. Not 'in' darkness/light — were, are. Identity precedes ethics.
Ps.37.3: Trust in the LORD, and do good; so shalt thou dwell in the land. Faith and obedience together produce settled inheritance. The verse anticipates the meek inheriting the earth (Matt 5:5).
Mal.4.2: Unto you that fear my name shall the Sun (shemesh) of righteousness arise with healing in His wings. Messianic prophecy. The healing is comprehensive — the Sun rises on God-fearers.
Ps.37.7: Rest (dom — be silent) in the LORD, and wait patiently (hith'cholel — twist with longing) for Him. Active waiting, not passive resignation. Fret not — the antidote to envy of the wicked.
Gal.3.13: Christ hath redeemed (exēgorasen — bought out from) us from the curse of the law, being made a curse for us. Cited Deut 21:23. The curse-bearing of substitution is explicit.
1Tim.3.16: Great is the mystery of godliness: God was manifest in the flesh, justified in the Spirit, seen of angels, preached unto the Gentiles, believed on in the world, received up into glory. Six-line Christ-hymn. Compresses the gospel into one verse.
Jude.1.24: Now unto Him that is able to keep you from falling (aptaistous — unstumbling), and to present you faultless before the presence of His glory. Doxology grounded in divine preservation. Christian assurance ultimately rests on Him.
Isa.7.14: A virgin (almah — young woman of marriageable age, traditionally virgin) shall conceive, and bear a son, and shall call His name Immanuel. Cited Matt 1:23 as fulfilled in the virgin conception of Christ. The double fulfillment (Isaiah's day and ultimate Messianic) is the classic interpretation.
Ps.62.8: Trust in Him at all times; ye people, pour out your heart before Him. The image: emptying a vessel completely. Prayer as emotional uncorking before God.
Heb.12.14: Follow peace with all men, and holiness, without which no man shall see the Lord. Two conditions, the second non-negotiable. The vision of God is morally conditioned.
Matt.5.8: Blessed are the pure in heart: for they shall see God. The vision of God is the supreme blessedness; purity of heart is its prerequisite. Inverts external religion of pharisaism.
Rom.1.16: I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ: for it is the power of God unto salvation. Power (dynamis) — the gospel itself is the divine instrument. To the Jew first, and also to the Greek — historic priority, not preferential love.
Eph.1.3: Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who hath blessed us with all spiritual blessings in heavenly places in Christ. The verbal triple-eulogion. All blessings located in Christ — outside Him, none.
Heb.3.7: Today if ye will hear His voice, harden not your hearts. Cited from Ps 95:7–8. The author cites the Holy Ghost (v. 7) as speaking the OT — Trinitarian view of inspiration.
Matt.1.23: Behold, a virgin shall be with child, and shall bring forth a son, and they shall call His name Emmanuel — God with us. Matthew quotes Isa 7:14, identifying its definitive fulfillment in Christ's virgin conception.
John.14.1: Let not your heart be troubled — present passive imperative, stop being troubled. Ye believe in God, believe also in me — co-equal object of trust. Christ's deity asserted at the upper room.
Isa.28.16: Behold, I lay in Zion for a foundation a stone, a tried stone, a precious cornerstone. Cited 1 Pet 2:6 of Christ. He that believeth shall not make haste (panic-flee).
Jer.6.16: Stand ye in the ways, and see, and ask for the old paths, where is the good way, and walk therein. The good way is ancient — established, time-tested. Pragmatic novelty is the temptation.
2Cor.4.6: God, who commanded the light to shine out of darkness — the Genesis 1 fiat — hath shined in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ. Conversion is a creation-act.
Prov.19.21: There are many devices in a man's heart; nevertheless the counsel of the LORD, that shall stand. Human plans are negotiable; divine counsel is final. The reassuring boundary on human planning.
Heb.10.22: Let us draw near with a true heart in full assurance of faith, having our hearts sprinkled from an evil conscience, and our bodies washed with pure water. Priestly access language now democratized to every believer through Christ.
1Thess.5.23: The very God of peace sanctify you wholly (holoteleis — completely); your whole spirit and soul and body be preserved blameless. Sanctification is comprehensive — every dimension of personhood.
Nah.1.7: The LORD is good, a strong hold (ma'oz) in the day of trouble; and He knoweth them that trust in Him. Even amid Nineveh's coming judgment, divine goodness toward refugees is asserted.
Heb.13.20: Now the God of peace, that brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus, that great Shepherd of the sheep, through the blood of the everlasting covenant. Trinitarian, Christocentric, covenantal benediction.
Josh.1.5: There shall not any man be able to stand before thee all the days of thy life. As I was with Moses, so I will be with thee. Quoted Heb 13:5 as still-applicable promise to believers.
Rev.21.6: It is done. I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end. I will give unto him that is athirst of the fountain of the water of life freely. Eschatological consummation language; the gift remains free even at the end.
Mark.7.6: This people honoureth me with their lips, but their heart is far from me. Cited Isa 29:13. Religious externalism without heart engagement is hypocrisy.
Rev.21.3: Behold, the tabernacle of God is with men, and He will dwell with them, and they shall be His people. The covenant goal across all of Scripture — Immanuel realized cosmically and eternally.
2Cor.4.16: Though our outward man perish, yet the inward man is renewed (anakainoutai — present passive) day by day. Two-track aging: outward decay, inward renewal. Sanctification continues even in physical decline.
Rom.5.3: We glory in tribulations also, knowing that tribulation worketh patience. Suffering's productive economy: tribulation → patience → experience → hope. Romans 5:3–5's chain.
2Cor.7.1: Let us cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit, perfecting (epitelountes) holiness in the fear of God. Sanctification is active and reverential.
Titus.2.11: The grace of God that bringeth salvation hath appeared (epephanē — was manifested) to all men. Saving grace as historical event in Christ's incarnation. The 'all men' — universal in offer, particular in efficacy.
2Pet.2.1: There were false prophets among the people, even as there shall be false teachers among you, who privily shall bring in damnable heresies. Pastoral warning. The presence of false teachers is predicted, not unexpected.
Eph.5.14: Awake thou that sleepest, and arise from the dead, and Christ shall give thee light. Possibly a fragment of an early Christian baptismal hymn. Conversion as resurrection.
Ps.57.5: Be thou exalted, O God, above the heavens; let thy glory be above all the earth. The refrain that closes both halves of Psalm 57. The doxological purpose of all things.
Ps.119.11: Thy word have I hid (tsafan — treasured) in mine heart, that I might not sin against thee. Scripture-internalization as moral preservation. The verse undergirds the discipline of memorization.
Rev.2.10: Be thou faithful unto death, and I will give thee a crown of life. The promise to persecuted Smyrna. Faithfulness through martyrdom is rewarded with eschatological life.
Hos.6.6: I desired mercy (chesed), and not sacrifice; and the knowledge of God more than burnt offerings. Cited by Jesus twice (Matt 9:13, 12:7). God's priority is relational, not ritual.
Heb.4.16: Let us therefore come boldly (parrēsias) unto the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy and find grace to help in time of need. Christ's priesthood opens audacious access. The throne is no longer terrifying — it is grace.
Rev.22.1: He shewed me a pure river of water of life, clear as crystal, proceeding out of the throne of God and of the Lamb. Eden restored and intensified. The new creation's central feature is the throne with its life-giving river.
Matt.7.13: Enter ye in at the strait gate; for wide is the gate, and broad is the way, that leadeth to destruction. Two-ways imagery. The narrow path is found by few — soteriological minority report.
Matt.11.29: Take my yoke upon you, and learn of me; for I am meek and lowly in heart: ye shall find rest unto your souls. Christ's yoke is true rest, not Pharisaical burden. Learn of me — discipleship is apprenticeship.
Gen.3.15: I will put enmity between thee and the woman, between thy seed and her seed; it shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel. The protoevangelium — the first gospel promise. Messianic crushing of the serpent foretold in the curse.
Ps.33.11: The counsel of the LORD standeth forever, the thoughts of His heart to all generations. Divine plans are unchanging across history. God's mind toward His people transcends epochs.
Gal.5.13: Brethren, ye have been called unto liberty; only use not liberty for an occasion to the flesh, but by love serve one another. Christian freedom is freedom for love-service, not license for self-service.
Zech.9.9: Rejoice greatly, O daughter of Zion: behold, thy King cometh unto thee — lowly, and riding upon an ass. Cited Matt 21:5 of the triumphal entry. Five-century-old prophecy fulfilled.
Ps.91.15: He shall call upon me, and I will answer him: I will be with him in trouble; I will deliver him, and honour him. Five divine actions on behalf of those who call. Promise of presence within trouble, not exemption from it.
John.6.63: It is the spirit that quickeneth; the flesh profiteth nothing: the words that I speak unto you, they are spirit, and they are life. Christ's words mediate the Spirit's life. Carnal interpretation misses everything.
1Cor.6.19: Know ye not that your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost which is in you, which ye have of God, and ye are not your own? Personal embodied stewardship. The Spirit's residence sanctifies the believer's body.
Rom.6.23: The wages of sin is death (earned), but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord (free). The contrast — wages vs. gift — highlights grace.
Ps.127.1: Except the LORD build the house, they labour in vain that build it. Divine sponsorship is necessary for human enterprise. Without God, all effort is futile.
Ezra.8.22: The hand of our God is upon all them for good that seek Him; but His power and His wrath is against all them that forsake Him. Two postures, two divine responses. Ezra's confidence as he traveled without armed escort.
1Thess.5.17: Pray without ceasing (adialeiptōs — without interruption). The Christian's life is set in an attitude of God-ward dialogue. Not literal monologue but constant openness.
